working-tree-staging-and-history.label: Record the First Community Project Snapshot
working-tree-staging-and-history.summary: Commit the staged two-file baseline and verify its history and clean state.

sketch2.title: Verify History and Current State Separately

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Record the First Community Snapshot
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt: The two project files are already staged. Record that proposal with the commit message `Start community project`. Do not restage or edit anything.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.hint: Commit the prepared staging area with the exact message.

help.concept: `git commit -m "Start community project"` records the current staging area as one local history entry. `-m` supplies the commit message directly. Only staged content enters the commit.
help.hint_1: Use the message exactly as written.
help.hint_2: After the command, the repository should contain one commit.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.starterCode:
# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.instructions: The two project files are already staged. Record that proposal with the commit message `Start community project`. Do not restage or edit anything.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- community-project
mkdir -p -- community-project
git -C community-project init -q -b main
git -C community-project config --local user.name 'ZoeSkoul Learner'
git -C community-project config --local user.email learner@zoeskoul.local
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
git -C community-project add -A
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you record the staged two-file snapshot with the requested message?

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Read the First History Entry
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt: Read the concise history and confirm that the newest entry is `Start community project`.

help.concept: `git log --oneline` lists commits newest first, showing a short identifier and the commit message on one line. It reads history without changing it.
help.hint_1: There should be exactly one history entry.
help.hint_2: The short identifier can differ; the message is what matters here.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.starterCode:
# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.instructions: Read the concise history and confirm that the newest entry is `Start community project`.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- community-project
mkdir -p -- community-project
git -C community-project init -q -b main
git -C community-project config --local user.name 'ZoeSkoul Learner'
git -C community-project config --local user.email learner@zoeskoul.local
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s '# Community Project

Shared community activities and updates.
' > community-project/README.md
mkdir -p -- community-project
printf %s 'Community events

Saturday cleanup: 10:00 AM
' > community-project/schedule.txt
git -C community-project add -A
GIT_AUTHOR_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z GIT_COMMITTER_DATE=2026-01-01T12:00:00Z git -C community-project commit -q -m 'Start community project'
find community-project -mindepth 1 -maxdepth 1 ! -name .git -exec rm -rf -- {} +

Did you read the concise commit history?

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Confirm a Clean Working State
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt: Inspect compact status after the first commit. A clean repository produces no file entries.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.hint: Read short status and look for an empty result.

help.concept: `git status --short` prints one line per unfinished path. No output means the working tree and staging area match the current commit.
help.hint_1: Do not create or edit a file.
help.hint_2: The repository should remain at one commit.

0.message: That action can discard or rewrite history. Use the safe, layer-specific workflow taught in this course.
Did you verify that no unfinished file entries remain?

single-choice-1.title: Identify the Working Tree
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the working tree in Git?
single-choice-1.hint: Choose the layer that contains the project files you can open and edit right now.

help.concept: Follow one edit from the working tree to the staging area and into a named commit, checking each boundary along the way.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: The project files and folders you can currently edit
options.b: The proposed snapshot prepared for the next commit
options.d: The list of patterns Git should ignore

single-choice-2.title: Purpose of the Staging Area
single-choice-2.prompt: What is the purpose of the staging area in Git?
single-choice-2.hint: Choose the layer where selected changes wait before they become a commit.

help.concept: Follow one edit from the working tree to the staging area and into a named commit, checking each boundary along the way.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To prepare selected changes for the next commit
options.b: To store the complete commit history
options.c: To display only unstaged line changes

multi-choice-1.title: What a Commit Records
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which facts about a commit are correct? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Follow one edit from the working tree to the staging area and into a named commit, checking each boundary along the way.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

options.b: It includes a message and author identity.
options.c: It automatically includes every unstaged edit.
options.d: It becomes part of repository history.

drag-reorder-1.title: Order of Git Workflow
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps of the Git workflow in the correct order.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Follow one edit from the working tree to the staging area and into a named commit, checking each boundary along the way.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

tokens.t1: Edit files in the working tree
tokens.t2: Stage changes in the staging area
tokens.t3: Commit changes to the commit history

working-tree-intro.title: Record the Prepared Snapshot
working-tree-intro.bodyMarkdown: The same two files remain staged from the previous lesson. `git commit -m "Start community project"` records exactly that staged proposal as the first history entry. The commit command does not automatically include later unstaged edits.

staging-area-intro.title: Read the History Entry
staging-area-intro.bodyMarkdown: `git log --oneline` lists commits newest first, one entry per line. Each line contains a short identifier and the commit message. The identifier may differ between valid repositories; the message explains the purpose of the snapshot.

commit-history-intro.title: Confirm the Current State
commit-history-intro.bodyMarkdown: `git status --short` prints one line for each unfinished path. When it prints nothing, the working tree and staging area match the current commit. History inspection and current-state inspection answer different questions.
